utterances: Well, all right.
We're going to start something new here.
So the Republican nominee, well, Scott Brown, he's a member of the state Senate.
He's going to face a very - how is he going to try to make up what is going to be a very substantial margin?
Well, independents sort of hold the balance of power in a general election.
They are about 51 percent of the state.
Democrats are 37 percent, and Republicans are 12 percent.
So you've got to appeal to the independents.
And Brown is a guy who probably wants to run for office again, even if he doesn't make it this time.
So he's got to run a campaign that's responsible, that allows him to run for, say, Martha Coakley's attorney general job if he loses, or there'll be an auditor's job open.
There'll be a treasurer's slot open in the state.
So he's got to run a campaign that will allow him to fight another day.
And does Coakley have to guard against being too cautious?
Yeah, I think so.

I think, you know, part of the problem for Brown - and I'm sure Coakley will take advantage of this - is that, you know, we're going to have two holidays falling between now and the general election, which is January 19th.
So there'll probably be a little more campaigning this week, a little bit next week, and then the campaign will shut down for a couple of weeks as the holidays come.
And it's going to be really hard for Brown to break through two of the six weeks in the general election.
One of Coakley's advantages in the primary was name recognition.
She was the only candidate who had run statewide before.
She'll have that same advantage in the general.
Absolutely.
People know her, they're comfortable with her.
I saw polls toward the end of this campaign that had her cruising at 70-plus percent favorability and a very low negative, maybe in the teens.
If anything, that probably grew more last night.
So Scott Brown's got a lot of ground to make up, and he didn't - you know, he had a primary, but he was running against a nut.
And so winning big, as he did, with 80-some percent of the vote was relatively meaningless.
And there weren't debates.
There was one kind of small confrontation toward the end of the campaign, but Brown never got a chance to explain who he is in debates.
And is he going to have any money?
That sort of depends on whether the national Republicans want him to run a well-funded race.

You know, I'd say if he doesn't get national money, he's going to find getting money in Massachusetts extremely difficult because Coakley is well-regarded in the legal community, and they give big bucks.
She regulates nonprofits as the attorney general.
So nonprofits will be actively involved in her campaign financially.
I think she'll be able to raise money very easily, and Brown will have a very hard time.
But Brown's got to worry about one thing, which is he can't come across as a national Republican.
The way Massachusetts Republicans who succeed run is by being fiscal conservatives and social moderates, and Brown already is not a social moderate.
He's opposed to gay marriage.
He's for the death penalty.
He has a number of positions on abortion.
No one knows quite what his position on abortion is.
He's a pretty hard-right Republican in a state that is very blue.
All right, Dan Payne, thanks very much.
I'm sure we'll be checking with you as the election approaches in January.
My pleasure.
Democratic media consultant Dan Payne joined us from the studios of our member station in Boston, WBUR.

We have some people on the line who think they know the answer to this week's trivia question, which again is: There is one state in the union in which a major party has never nominated a female to run for United States Senate or for governor, much less elected one.
If you think you know the answer, 800-989-8255.
Email is talk@npr.org.
We'll start with Jennifer(ph), Jennifer with us from Tallahassee.
Is it Florida?
Well, Florida, no because Paula Hawkins was a senator in the 1980s.
As a matter of fact, Paula Hawkins died this week, Florida's first female senator, but she was elected in 1980 in the Reagan landslide.
So it's not Florida.
Okay, thanks.
Thanks for the call, Jennifer.
Let's see if we can go next to Shelley(ph) with us from Athens - is this Athens, Ohio or Georgia?
Athens, Ohio.
Go ahead, please.
I only heard the governor part, but I was going to say Ohio.
Well, no because also, if you remember, Mary Boyle was the Democratic nominee against Senator Voinovich a couple years back, I think it was 1998.
So you did have a female nominee for the Senate.
Yeah, I missed that part of the question.
Thanks.
Okay, Shelley, thanks very much.
Let's go next to Scott(ph), Scott with us from Oklahoma City.
Hi, yeah, is it Oklahoma?
Oklahoma?
Well, no because there was a woman named Laura Boyd who ran for governor against Frank Keating in 1988.
Okay, I thought - you're right, you're right.
Yeah, I forgot all about that.
Oh, don't you hate it when Ken is right?
I do.
All right.
Thanks very much for the call, Scott.
And we'll go next to Mark(ph), and Mark's with us, calling from the Badlands of South Dakota.
Hey, guys.
Ken's almost always right, you know.
I know.
Almost, wow.
Everybody's sticking with their home states here, and I've got to go with South Dakota.
No incumbent, no senator, no governor but also no nominee for the Senate or governor.
Mary's on the line from Aiken, South Carolina.
Is it South Carolina?
Well, just a few years ago, Inez Tenenbaum, you remember that name, ran against Jim DeMint for the Senate.
So it's not South Carolina.
All right, thanks.
Nice try, Mary.
Let's see if we can go to Sharon(ph), and Sharon's with us from Salt Lake City.
Iowa?
No, not Iowa because there was a - let's see, I'm trying to think.
There was Roxanne Conlin, who as you well remember, ran for governor against Terry Branstad in the 1980s, I believe.
Well, at least she picked not her home state, anyway.
Nice - thanks for the call, Sharon.
And let's see if we can squeeze in one more before we have to go to the break, and this is Mike(ph), and Mike's with us also from Aiken, South Carolina.
And I think my guess was South Carolina, and that's what the person ahead of me said.
And it's still wrong.
Oh well, we're backward in everything else.
I just figured it had to be South Carolina.
Well, Ken, we just have a few seconds left.
I think - wait a minute, we've got one more caller in.
Let's go to Mike(ph), Mike with us from Marcy in New York.
Yeah, how's it going?
Good.
What's your guess?
My guess is Hawaii.
